title: Wet-felted nuno cowl
subtitle: A wide nuno-felted neck cowl on open-weave silk, approximately 50 × 45 cm, in merino and silk with a gathered, textured surface
excerpt: A wide cowl nuno-felted from merino roving onto an open-weave silk scrim base. As the merino shrinks and bonds through the silk weave, the silk gathers into a deeply textured surface. The finished cowl wraps twice around the neck or sits as a wide collar.
sourceNotes: Nuno felting on silk scrim is an established technique for creating lightweight felted textiles with a gathered surface. Method synthesised from general nuno-felting practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique in which merino wool fibres are felted onto an open-weave fabric base. The fibres migrate through the fabric and lock to create a lightweight, drapey, textured fabric.
A loosely woven fabric with a visible open weave used as the base fabric in nuno felting. Open-weave silk scrim allows wool fibres to migrate through the structure and lock in place.
Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, the material felted onto the fabric base in nuno felting.
Vigorous agitation that shrinks the wool fibres, gathering the fabric they are attached to into the characteristic textured surface of nuno felt.

content.text: A nuno cowl is lighter and more drapey than a fully wet-felted neck piece. The
nuno-felting
process bonds wool
roving
through the open weave of a silk
scrim
base: as the wool shrinks during
fulling

, it pulls the silk into deep gathers and pleats. The resulting fabric has a textured, three-dimensional surface and the warmth and softness of merino with the sheen of silk. The cowl is shaped as a wide rectangle that wraps around the neck comfortably twice.

items.name: Merino roving, contrasting colour (optional surface detail)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Lay the silk and apply roving

Lay the felting mat on a flat surface. Spread the silk scrim flat on the mat. Pull thin wisps of merino roving and lay them across the silk in one direction only (horizontal), covering approximately 70% of the silk surface. Leave areas of the silk uncovered between roving wisps: the uncovered silk will gather freely and create the textured effect. Add a second layer of roving at 90 degrees over the first, again covering approximately 70% of the surface. Do not build up a solid even layer as in standard wet felting: the sparse coverage is intentional.
Step 2: Wet down

Mix soapy water (40°C). Drizzle slowly over the roving and silk until both are saturated. Place the felting net over the surface. Press down with flat hands to push out air. The roving will flatten against the silk and the soapy water will penetrate the silk weave.
Step 3: Rub gently at first

Rub through the felting net with flat hands using small circular movements, beginning with very light pressure. Nuno felting requires patience at this stage: if you rub too hard before the fibres begin to bond through the silk, the roving slides across the surface without migrating. After 5 minutes at light pressure, increase to medium pressure for a further 8 minutes. The roving should begin to grip the silk: test by lifting the net and trying to slide a wisp of roving sideways with a fingertip. When the roving resists sliding, the fibres have begun to migrate through the weave.
Step 4: Roll and full

Flip the piece over and rub the back of the silk gently for 5 minutes to encourage fibres to fully lock through the weave. Then roll the piece in the bamboo mat and roll firmly back and forth for 5 minutes per direction, rotating 90 degrees each time. The silk will begin to gather visibly as the wool shrinks. Continue rolling and re-wetting for 20-30 minutes until the piece has shrunk to approximately 55 × 45 cm and the surface has a pronounced gathered texture.
Step 5: Throw and finalise

Throw the cowl against the felting mat ten to fifteen times to complete the fulling of the wool fibres. Rinse in cool water until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently without wringing. Smooth the cowl into a rectangle and leave flat on a towel to dry. The gathers will set as the piece dries.

items.problem: The roving slides across the silk surface during rubbing rather than migrating through the weave
items.cause: Rubbing pressure was applied before the fibres had a chance to begin the migration process
items.fix: Reduce pressure to the lightest possible touch for the first 10 minutes. The fibres need time to swell in the warm soapy water and begin catching in the weave structure before they can withstand directional rubbing.

items.problem: The surface gathers very little and the cowl remains almost flat
items.cause: The roving coverage was too dense, preventing the silk from gathering freely
items.fix: In the next project, reduce the roving to a single sparse layer covering no more than 50-60% of the silk. More gathering occurs where the silk can move freely between roving anchors.

items.problem: The silk weave shows as an obvious grid pattern rather than blending with the wool
items.cause: The silk scrim has too open a weave for the roving weight used
items.fix: Use a finer merino (17-18 micron) over an open-weave scrim. Finer fibres migrate through smaller weave openings and produce a more integrated surface.

content.text: Care

Hand-wash in cool water with a silk-and-wool-safe detergent. Press flat between two dry towels to remove moisture. Dry flat. Do not wring, tumble dry, or hang while wet: the gathered silk will distort under its own weight if the cowl is hung damp.
